reference: GATE CS — Compiler Design / Frontend Phases

map.condition: Question asks about compiler phase that reads source character-by-character
Question asks which automaton / data structure a phase uses
Question asks to count tokens in a C statement
map.use: \text{Count: keywords + identifiers + literals + operators + punctuators (not whitespace)}

map.condition: Question involves macro expansion order or recursion
map.use: \text{Macro expanded at use site (call by name); recursive macro} \Rightarrow \text{infinite loop possible}

map.condition: Question about symbol table property or implementation
map.use: \text{Symbol Table: scope, type, size, address; implemented via hash table, BST, or linked list}

formulas.name: Standard Compiler Phase Sequence
formulas.plain: Source -> Lexical(FA) -> Syntax(PDA) -> Semantic(Attr.Grammar) -> Opt(DAG) -> CodeGen(Graph) -> Object
formulas.whenToUse: When matching each compiler phase to its underlying automaton or data structure

terms.means: Finite Automaton — used by lexer to recognise tokens
Push-Down Automaton — used by parser to check syntax
Directed Acyclic Graph — used in code optimisation to eliminate redundancy

formulas.trap: Confusing DAG (optimisation) with syntax tree (semantic analysis); code generation uses register-allocation graphs, not DAGs

formulas.name: Role of Lexical Analyser
formulas.latex: \text{Lexical Analyser: reads every char of source} \Rightarrow \text{emits } \langle \textit{token-type},\ \textit{attribute} \rangle
formulas.plain: Lexical Analyser: reads every char of source => emits <token-type, attribute>
formulas.whenToUse: When question asks which module checks every character or produces tokens

terms.means: category: keyword, identifier, literal, operator, punctuator
pointer into symbol table or value of literal

formulas.trap: Syntax analyser does NOT read characters; it reads tokens produced by the lexer

formulas.name: Which Phase Detects Which Error
formulas.latex: \begin{array}{ll} \text{Lexical error} & \text{(invalid char / malformed token)} \\ \text{Syntax error} & \text{(grammar rule violation)} \\ \text{Semantic error} & \text{(type mismatch, undeclared var)} \\ \text{Logical error} & \text{(not caught by compiler)} \end{array}
formulas.plain: Lexical error: invalid char / malformed token | Syntax error: grammar violation | Semantic error: type mismatch or undeclared var | Logical error: not caught
formulas.whenToUse: When question gives a buggy source line (e.g. 'fro' instead of 'for') and asks which phase catches it

terms.means: Misspelling of 'for' — lexer emits it as a valid identifier; syntax error is caught by parser

formulas.trap: A typo keyword like 'fro' is a VALID identifier token for the lexer; the syntax error is caught by the parser, not the lexer

formulas.name: Single-Pass vs. Multi-Pass Compiler
formulas.latex: \text{Single-pass: one scan of source} \quad \text{Multi-pass: } k \text{ scans, each a pass}
formulas.plain: Single-pass: one scan of source | Multi-pass: k scans, each a pass
formulas.whenToUse: When question asks about number of passes or when a task (e.g. macro expansion) is done

terms.means: a complete scan over the source or intermediate representation

formulas.trap: In a two-pass macro assembler, macro expansion is done in pass 1 (definition table built and calls expanded) so pass 2 has clean assembly code

formulas.name: Token Categories
formulas.plain: Tokens = { keywords, identifiers, literals, operators, punctuators }
formulas.whenToUse: When counting tokens in a C/language statement

terms.means: reserved word: if, int, for, printf, etc.
user-defined names: variable names, function names
constant values including string literals like "i=%d, &i=%x"

formulas.trap: Whitespace and comments are NOT tokens; a string literal like "i=%d, &i=%x" is ONE token, not multiple

formulas.name: Token Count: printf Statement
formulas.plain: printf("i=%d, &i=%x", i, &i); => 10 tokens: printf ( "i=%d,&i=%x" , i , & i ) ;
formulas.whenToUse: Direct GATE question: count tokens in this exact C statement

terms.means: 1 identifier token
1 string-literal token
each occurrence is 1 identifier token
address-of operator token

formulas.trap: Characters inside a string literal are NOT separate tokens; count the whole string as ONE token

formulas.name: Effect of Identifier Length on Compilation
formulas.latex: \text{Longer identifiers} \Rightarrow \text{same # tokens, same compile time (lexer hashes them)}
formulas.plain: Longer identifiers => same number of tokens, effectively same compile time
formulas.whenToUse: When asked whether longer identifiers slow down compilation or change program correctness

terms.means: lexer uses hashing to look up identifiers in symbol table in O(1)

formulas.trap: Longer identifiers do NOT necessarily slow compilation and do NOT make a program incorrect; they may improve readability

formulas.name: Regular Expression -> NFA -> DFA Pipeline
formulas.whenToUse: When question involves constructing a lexer from token patterns specified as regular expressions

terms.means: Nondeterministic Finite Automaton — may have epsilon transitions
Deterministic Finite Automaton — one transition per symbol per state
Minimized DFA — fewest states, used in efficient lexers

formulas.trap: NFA and DFA recognise the SAME class (regular languages); minimisation reduces states but not expressive power

formulas.name: Tokens Are Regular Languages
formulas.plain: Token patterns are regular languages => recognisable by Finite Automaton
formulas.whenToUse: When question asks why lexer uses FA and not PDA or TM

terms.means: described by a regular expression; no counting/nesting needed

formulas.trap: Nested constructs (balanced parentheses) are NOT regular and require a PDA — they belong to the parser, not lexer

formulas.name: Macro Definition Structure
formulas.plain: MACRO name p1, p2, ..., pn  <body>  ENDM  — p1..pn are formal parameters
formulas.whenToUse: When asked to identify formal vs. actual parameters in a macro definition

terms.means: placeholder variable in macro definition (x, y in 'macro Add x, y')
value/expression supplied at macro call site

formulas.trap: Macro parameters are FORMAL in the definition; they become actual only at the call site — unlike function arguments the substitution is textual (call by name)

formulas.name: Macro Expansion — Call by Name (Textual Substitution)
formulas.latex: \text{Macro call} \xrightarrow{\text{preprocessor}} \text{textual substitution of body with actual args}
formulas.plain: Macro call => preprocessor substitutes body with actual arguments (textual, not evaluated)
formulas.whenToUse: When question distinguishes macro expansion from function call

terms.means: actual argument text replaces formal parameter — not evaluated first

formulas.trap: Macros are expanded by preprocessor/assembler, NOT at run time; side-effects in actual args may be evaluated multiple times

formulas.name: Two-Pass Assembler: Macro Expansion in Pass 1
formulas.latex: \text{Pass 1: build macro def table + expand all calls} \Rightarrow \text{Pass 2 sees pure assembly (no macros)}
formulas.plain: Pass 1: build macro definition table + expand all macro calls => Pass 2: only pure assembly instructions
formulas.whenToUse: When asked why macro expansion is done in pass 1, not pass 2, of a two-pass assembler

terms.means: first scan: collects labels and macro definitions, expands macro calls
second scan: resolves addresses, generates object code

formulas.trap: If expansion were left to pass 2, the expanded code would introduce new labels that pass 1 already missed, causing forward-reference errors

formulas.name: Recursive Macro — Infinite Loop Condition
formulas.latex: \text{Recursive macro} M \text{ with no base case} \Rightarrow \text{infinite expansion loop}
formulas.plain: Recursive macro M with no terminating base case => infinite macro expansion loop
formulas.whenToUse: When question asks which macro definition can cause an infinite loop in a macro assembler

terms.means: conditional branch that stops recursion when a termination condition is met

formulas.trap: A macro that calls itself conditionally is safe only if the condition is guaranteed to become false; if the condition is never false the assembler loops infinitely

formulas.name: Symbol Table — Purpose & Contents
formulas.plain: Symbol Table entry: <name, type, scope, size, address>
formulas.whenToUse: When question asks what information the symbol table stores or which data structure manages variable attributes

terms.means: block/function in which the identifier is visible
int, float, pointer, array, etc.
memory location assigned during code generation

formulas.trap: The symbol table is NOT only used by the lexer; it is read and written across multiple phases (lexer, parser, semantic analyser, code generator)

formulas.name: Symbol Table — Implementation Choices
formulas.latex: \text{Hash Table: } O(1)\text{ avg} \quad \text{BST: } O(\log n) \quad \text{Linked List: } O(n)
formulas.plain: Hash Table: O(1) avg | BST: O(log n) | Linked List: O(n)
formulas.whenToUse: When question asks about valid or efficient implementations of the symbol table

terms.means: number of identifiers declared in the program

formulas.trap: The symbol table can be implemented with BST or hash table; the statement 'only hash tables are valid' is FALSE — BSTs and linked lists also work

formulas.name: Scope Management with Symbol Table Stack
formulas.plain: Enter scope => push new symbol table; Exit scope => pop symbol table
formulas.whenToUse: When question involves nested scopes, block-structured languages, or shadowing of variable names

terms.means: stack of symbol tables, innermost on top; lookup searches from top downward

formulas.trap: Destroying the symbol table after compilation is optional; some compilers retain it for debugging information

formulas.name: Identifier Pattern (C-style)
formulas.plain: identifier = [a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z_0-9]*  (starts with letter or underscore, followed by letters/digits/underscores)
formulas.whenToUse: When asked to write or evaluate a regex for a valid C identifier

terms.means: first character: any letter or underscore
subsequent characters: zero or more letters, digits, or underscores

formulas.trap: Identifiers cannot START with a digit; '2var' is not a valid identifier — lexer would tokenise '2' as a numeric literal and 'var' as a separate identifier

formulas.name: Integer Literal Pattern
formulas.whenToUse: When constructing NFA/DFA for integer constants in lexer design questions

terms.means: one or more decimal digit characters

formulas.trap: An integer literal pattern must be [0-9]+ (one or more), NOT [0-9]* (zero or more would match the empty string)
